intro:
Scripture names a small number of chief angels who stand in God’s presence and carry out his greatest errands. Only Michael is explicitly called an archangel (Jude 1:9; cf. 1 Thessalonians 4:16), and only Gabriel and Michael are named in the protocanonical books; Raphael appears in the book of Tobit. Together they represent the highest rank of the angelic host — messengers, warriors, and guardians of God’s people.

The chief angels

Michael — the archangel and "great prince" who guards God’s people and wars against the dragon (Daniel 12:1; Revelation 12:7).

Gabriel — the heavenly messenger who interpreted Daniel’s visions and announced the births of John and of Jesus (Luke 1:19; Luke 1:26–33).

Raphael — named in the book of Tobit as one of the seven holy angels who stand before the glory of the Lord (deuterocanonical).

link_tables.title: The Archangels

cells: Warrior-prince who guards God's people and contends with the dragon
Herald who announced the births of John and Jesus
Named in Tobit as one of the seven who stand before God

link_tables.note: Only Michael is called "archangel" (Jude 1:9). Click a name to read more.
